steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1's claim: *The Known World* is too rich to be called "historical fiction" because that label implies shallow "mere re-creation." How would Text 2's author respond to this?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'by arguing Jones displays thorough knowledge of historical details': Text 2 explicitly says critics wrongly reduce historical fiction to "showing off knowledge of period details." Praising Jones for that would align with the shallow view Text 2 rejects. ✗
  • Choice B — 'by claiming Text 1 underestimated the richness of *The Known World*': Text 1 praises *The Known World* as "richer and more complicated." Text 2's author would have no reason to say Text 1 underestimates it. ✗
  • Choice C — 'by conceding historical fiction contains many works less sophisticated than *The Known World*': Text 2 defends historical fiction broadly — it does not concede that the genre is often shallow. ✗
  • Choice D — 'by asserting that dissatisfaction with the label reflects a misunderstanding of the genre': Text 2 argues critics misunderstand historical fiction — it can explore profound themes and complex characters. Text 1's discomfort with the label stems from seeing historical fiction as inherently shallow, exactly the misunderstanding Text 2 corrects. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
